## [03:00 - 06:05]

And so if you can link it to a physical experience or something they can do in an action or a relationship or something concrete, then it gives them more control over the emotion. So the physical stress that would feel going through several Gs of, you know, high-speed flight was going to be a contrast to the type of stress that they handle on a regular basis. So it's the challenge of the experience of physical stress. And the purpose is to experience stress as something that makes you stronger. ## [18:10 - 21:13]

And so to stop blaming, you want to start questioning. And there's a difference when we experience being upset with another person, we're judging them and we're trying to control them. And so one thing that we teach that's really important is that people have two frameworks in which they relate to people. These are just two, not all of them, but these are two. Basically, people try to control someone, and then they think that their judgment is going to change them, or they try to force them, or they try to negotiate with them to do something, rather than that's called a control frame. A control frame is like, I'm going to take control of you and I'm going to make you do this. But the opposite is the influence frame, which is that you're going to just be very curious and understand what acts of that person actually responds to, and you look at it from their point of view. If I were them, how would I want to see it? What would I respond to? What would I act on? And you're more experimental and more inquisitive and more curious in your approach.
